text:
COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT
FoundationOne CDx

-------------------------------------------------------------------
PATIENT INFORMATION
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Patient Name: Raymond Kowalski
Date of Birth: [on file]
Sex: Male
Diagnosis: Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma, WHO/ISUP Grade 4, with Sarcomatoid Differentiation
Primary Site: Right Kidney
Specimen Type: Core Needle Biopsy (CT-guided), Right Renal Mass
Specimen ID: RK-CNB-2024-0041
Tumor Content (estimated): 65% (pathologist reviewed)
Ordering Physician: [Oncology/Urology Attending]
Institution: [Academic Cancer Center]

-------------------------------------------------------------------
TEST METHODOLOGY
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Platform: FoundationOne CDx (Foundation Medicine, Inc.)
Assay Type: Hybrid capture-based next-generation sequencing (NGS)
Genomic Coverage: 324 cancer-relevant genes (full exonic sequencing); select introns for structural variants; genome-wide copy number analysis
Sequencing Depth: Mean coverage >500x (tumor); minimum 250x coverage threshold applied
Analysis: Somatic alterations reported; matched germline filtering applied using peripheral blood leukocyte DNA
Regulatory Status: FDA-approved companion diagnostic
Quality Control: All QC metrics met; tumor content confirmed adequate for analysis

-------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY OF GENOMIC FINDINGS
-------------------------------------------------------------------
A total of NINE genomic alterations were identified in this tumor specimen, including four clinically actionable alterations and five additional variants of established biological significance. Chromosomal copy number analysis reveals a profile consistent with advanced clear cell renal cell carcinoma. No microsatellite instability (MSI-High) was detected. Tumor mutational burden is low.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
ACTIONABLE / CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT ALTERATIONS
-------------------------------------------------------------------

1. VHL -- p.L89fs*4 (c.265_266delCT)
   Variant Type: Frameshift deletion
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Loss of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 48%
   Exon: 2
   Zygosity: Consistent with biallelic inactivation (heterozygous deletion + 3p LOH)
   Clinical Significance: VHL is the most commonly inactivated tumor suppressor in clear cell RCC, identified in up to 80-90% of sporadic cases. Loss of VHL protein function leads to constitutive stabilization of hypoxia-inducible factors (HIF-1alpha and HIF-2alpha) and subsequent upregulation of VEGF, PDGF, and other pro-angiogenic targets. This frameshift mutation at codon 89 introduces a premature stop codon (p.L89fs*4) predicted to result in a severely truncated, non-functional protein. Biallelic inactivation is confirmed by concurrent chromosome 3p loss encompassing the VHL locus. This alteration is the founding genomic event in this tumor and provides the mechanistic rationale for VEGF-targeted therapies (Sutent, Votrient, axitinib, cabozantinib, bev). Note: No co-occurring HIF2A mutation was detected; HIF2alpha inhibition (belzutifan) would require separate clinical assessment.

2. PBRM1 -- p.Q357* (c.1069C>T)
   Variant Type: Nonsense mutation
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Loss of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 42%
   Exon: 9
   Zygosity: Consistent with biallelic inactivation (3p LOH)
   Clinical Significance: PBRM1 (Polybromo-1, also known as BAF180) encodes a core subunit of the PBAF SWI/SNF chromatin-remodeling complex. PBRM1 is the second most commonly mutated gene in clear cell RCC (mutated in ~40% of cases), functioning as a tumor suppressor whose loss contributes to transcriptional dysregulation and chromatin accessibility changes. This nonsense mutation (p.Q357*) within the second bromodomain of PBRM1 results in a truncated, non-functional protein. Loss-of-function PBRM1 mutations have been associated with response to PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint blockade in clear cell RCC, potentially through enhanced immunogenicity (Miao et al., Science 2018). However, the co-occurrence of BAP1 loss (see below) partially offsets this favorable predictive association and is concordant with higher tumor grade and sarcomatoid features in this specimen.

3. BAP1 -- p.R227* (c.679C>T)
   Variant Type: Nonsense mutation
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Loss of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 39%
   Exon: 7
   Zygosity: Consistent with biallelic inactivation (3p LOH)
   Clinical Significance: BAP1 (BRCA1-Associated Protein 1) encodes a deubiquitinase that functions as a nuclear tumor suppressor. BAP1 is located on chromosome 3p21.1 and is lost in approximately 10-15% of clear cell RCC cases. Notably, BAP1-mutant clear cell RCCs are associated with significantly higher tumor grade (WHO/ISUP grade 3-4), increased propensity for sarcomatoid differentiation, larger tumor size, and inferior clinical outcomes compared to BAP1-wild type tumors. The concurrent loss of both PBRM1 and BAP1 -- while uncommon, given these genes are typically co-deleted from 3p in a mutually exclusive pattern in some studies -- is observed here and is consistent with this tumor's aggressive phenotype (grade 4, 22% sarcomatoid differentiation, extensive tumor necrosis). This finding may serve as a biomarker of aggressive biology and should inform prognosis and management planning. BAP1 loss has also been reported to associate with enhanced sensitivity to mTOR inhibitor-based therapy in some preclinical and clinical datasets.

4. MET -- Amplification (Copy Number: 6.8 copies; Fold-change: ~3.4x relative to ploidy-adjusted normal)
   Variant Type: Copy Number Amplification
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Gain of Function
   Clinical Significance: MET encodes a receptor tyrosine kinase (hepatocyte growth factor receptor) whose amplification drives oncogenic signaling via RAS/ERK, PI3K/AKT, and Stat3 pathways, promoting cell proliferation, survival, invasion, and metastasis. MET amplification is an uncommon but established alteration in advanced RCC and has been associated with VEGF TKI resistance as well as potential sensitivity to MET-targeting agents. Cabozantinib (XL184), a multi-targeted TKI with potent activity against VEGFR2, MET, and AXL, has demonstrated superior efficacy over suni and Afinitor in patients with MET-dysregulated RCC (METEOR trial, CABOSUN trial data). The presence of MET amplification in this specimen provides a rationale for cabozantinib as a preferred component of the therapeutic strategy. Additionally, MET amplification may promote the invasive and metastatic phenotype observed in this tumor (renal vein thrombus, retroperitoneal nodal spread).

-------------------------------------------------------------------
ADDITIONAL VARIANTS OF BIOLOGICAL SIGNIFICANCE
-------------------------------------------------------------------

5. SETD2 -- p.R1625* (c.4873C>T)
   Variant Type: Nonsense mutation
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Loss of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 31%
   Exon: 21
   Clinical Significance: SETD2 encodes the only non-redundant H3K36 trimethyltransferase in mammalian cells. Located at chromosome 3p21.31, SETD2 is co-deleted with VHL, PBRM1, and BAP1 via 3p loss and is additionally mutated somatically in up to 12% of clear cell RCCs. Loss of SETD2 function results in loss of H3K36me3, leading to aberrant transcription, splicing errors, impaired DNA mismatch repair, and genomic instability. SETD2 loss has been implicated in intratumoral heterogeneity and may contribute to clonal evolution and drug resistance. No direct therapeutic implications at this time, but SETD2 loss may enhance sensitivity to certain DNA-damaging agents.

6. KDM5C -- p.Y611* (c.1833C>G)
   Variant Type: Nonsense mutation
   Variant Classification: Likely Pathogenic -- Loss of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 22%
   Exon: 15
   Clinical Significance: KDM5C (also known as JARID1C) is an X-linked histone H3 lysine 4 (H3K4) demethylase implicated in epigenetic regulation of gene transcription. KDM5C mutations are identified in approximately 7-10% of clear cell RCCs and have been reported to contribute to epigenetic dysregulation in this cancer type. The lower VAF (22%) compared to PBRM1 and BAP1 suggests this may represent a subclonal event, potentially arising later in tumor evolution. KDM5C loss may contribute to transcriptional heterogeneity and altered immune microenvironment.

7. CDKN2A / CDKN2B -- Homozygous Deletion (Chromosome 9p21.3)
   Variant Type: Homozygous Copy Number Loss
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Loss of Function (both alleles)
   Clinical Significance: Homozygous deletion of the CDKN2A/CDKN2B locus on chromosome 9p21 results in complete loss of p16(INK4a), p14(ARF), and p15(INK4b), which are critical regulators of the G1/S cell cycle checkpoint and ARF-MDM2-p53 tumor suppressor axis. Homozygous deletion of this locus is associated with aggressive tumor behavior, accelerated cell cycle progression, and resistance to CDK4/6 inhibition. In the context of clear cell RCC, CDKN2A homozygous deletion has been associated with higher-grade tumors and shorter progression-free survival on standard therapy. This deletion may partially explain the aggressive grade 4 phenotype, extensive sarcomatoid differentiation, and rapid clinical course observed in this patient.

8. TP53 -- p.R248W (c.742C>T)
   Variant Type: Missense mutation
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Gain of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 18%
   Exon: 7
   Germline/Somatic: Somatic (germline filtering performed; no germline TP53 alteration identified)
   Clinical Significance: TP53 p.R248W is one of the most well-characterized gain-of-function (GOF) TP53 hotspot mutations, resulting in a dominant negative p53 protein that not only fails to suppress tumor growth but actively promotes oncogenesis through aberrant transcriptional activation of proliferative genes and inhibition of wild-type p53 in neighboring cells. TP53 mutations are relatively uncommon in clear cell RCC (5-10%) but when present are associated with worse outcomes. The subclonal VAF (18%) suggests this may represent a later subclonal event, possibly arising in the sarcomatoid component or during metastatic progression. TP53 GOF mutations may confer resistance to certain therapies and can activate PI3K/AKT and MAPK pathway signaling independent of upstream oncogenic drivers.

9. TERT Promoter -- C228T (chr5:1,295,228 C>T)
   Variant Type: Promoter mutation
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Gain of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 35%
   Clinical Significance: The TERT promoter C228T mutation (also denoted C>T at position -124 relative to the ATG start site, creating a de novo ETS/GABPA transcription factor binding site) results in transcriptional upregulation of telomerase reverse transcriptase, leading to telomere maintenance and replicative immortality. TERT promoter mutations are found across multiple cancer types and are observed in approximately 10-15% of RCCs. Their presence is associated with advanced-stage disease and unfavorable prognosis. In this context, the relatively high VAF (35%) suggests a clonal event. TERT promoter mutations may synergize with CDKN2A deletion in bypassing cellular senescence checkpoints.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
COPY NUMBER ANALYSIS
-------------------------------------------------------------------

Whole-genome copy number profiling reveals the following structural alterations:

- Chromosome 3p LOSS (arm-level): Loss encompassing 3p11.2-3p26.3
  - Includes loci: VHL (3p25.3), PBRM1 (3p21.3), BAP1 (3p21.1), SETD2 (3p21.31)
  - This is the canonical chromosome 3p deletion observed in the majority of clear cell RCCs and represents the sentinel event in this tumor's genomic evolution.

- Chromosome 9p LOSS (arm-level): Loss encompassing 9p21.3-9pter
  - Includes CDKN2A/CDKN2B homozygous deletion (confirmed by SNP-based allelic analysis)
  - Also includes IFNA/IFNB gene cluster at 9p21.3 (potential immunological implications)

- Chromosome 14q LOSS (arm-level): Loss encompassing 14q11.1-14q32.33
  - Includes HIF1A (14q23.2) -- note: no activating mutation in HIF1A identified; loss may reflect downstream transcriptional consequences
  - VHL/HIF pathway alterations combined with 14q loss is a common co-occurring pattern in high-grade clear cell RCC

- Chromosome 5q GAIN (arm-level): Gain encompassing 5q11.1-5q35.3
  - 5q gain is one of the most common copy number gains in clear cell RCC and has been associated with upregulation of oncogenes in this region
  - Does not contain known actionable therapeutic targets in this context

- MET (7q31) Focal Amplification: Copy number 6.8; high-level amplification relative to ploidy baseline

- No high-level amplifications in VEGFR1 (FLT1), VEGFR2 (KDR), EGFR, ERBB2, CDK4, CDK6, or MDM2
- No evidence of BRCA1 or BRCA2 copy number loss or mutation

-------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSATELLITE STATUS AND TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN
-------------------------------------------------------------------

Microsatellite Status: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
  - Analysis of 95 microsatellite loci; no evidence of microsatellite instability
  - MSS status indicates that immune checkpoint inhibitor response via MMR-deficiency pathway (as seen in MSI-H tumors) is not operative; however, PD-L1 expression and other immune microenvironment factors remain relevant predictive biomarkers

Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB): 6.2 mutations/Mb (LOW)
  - TMB threshold for TMB-High designation: >=10 mutations/Mb (FDA companion diagnostic threshold)
  - This tumor does not meet TMB-High criteria; pembro indication for TMB-High solid tumors does not apply
  - Low TMB in clear cell RCC is expected and consistent with known mutational landscape of this histology

-------------------------------------------------------------------
IMMUNE BIOMARKER
-------------------------------------------------------------------

PD-L1 Expression (Immunohistochemistry, 22C3 PharmDx assay, run concurrently on biopsy specimen):
  Combined Positive Score (CPS): 18
  Tumor Proportion Score (TPS): Approximately 12%
  Interpretation: PD-L1 CPS 18 indicates moderate-to-high PD-L1 expression, predominantly in tumor-associated immune cells. This level of PD-L1 expression is consistent with potential benefit from PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint blockade. In clear cell RCC, PD-L1 expression has been associated with higher tumor grade, sarcomatoid differentiation, and -- in combination with clinical factors -- may serve as a positive predictive biomarker for immune checkpoint inhibitor-based regimens such as Opdivo plus ipi or Keytruda plus axitinib.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
ALTERATIONS NOT DETECTED (RELEVANT NEGATIVES)
-------------------------------------------------------------------

- HIF2A (EPAS1): No activating mutation detected -- belzutifan (Welireg) targeted therapy not indicated by this finding
- TSC1 / TSC2: No pathogenic alterations detected -- mTOR pathway hyperactivation via TSC1/TSC2 loss not present
- NF2: No mutation detected
- FGFR1, FGFR2, FGFR3, FGFR4: No mutations, fusions, or amplifications detected
- KRAS, NRAS, HRAS: No mutations detected
- BRAF: No V600E or other activating mutations detected
- ALK: No fusion detected
- ROS1: No fusion detected
- RET: No fusion detected
- PIK3CA: No activating mutations detected
- MTOR: No activating mutations detected
- PTEN: No deletion or mutation detected
- BRCA1, BRCA2: No pathogenic variants detected (germline-filtered)
- STK11: No mutation detected
- KEAP1: No mutation detected
- IDH1, IDH2: No mutations detected

-------------------------------------------------------------------
GENOMIC EVOLUTION AND CLONAL ARCHITECTURE INTERPRETATION
-------------------------------------------------------------------

Integrating VAF data, copy number changes, and known patterns of clear cell RCC evolution, the following clonal structure is inferred:

Clonal (founding) events (high VAF, consistent with 3p LOH copy-neutral or deletion):
  - VHL p.L89fs*4 (VAF 48%) -- founding tumor suppressor event
  - PBRM1 p.Q357* (VAF 42%) -- early subclonal or near-clonal event following VHL loss
  - BAP1 p.R227* (VAF 39%) -- early co-occurring event; co-loss of PBRM1 and BAP1 is unusual and indicates a particularly aggressive genomic evolution
  - TERT promoter C228T (VAF 35%) -- clonal event enabling telomere maintenance and replicative immortality
  - SETD2 p.R1625* (VAF 31%) -- likely clonal; consistent with 3p LOH

Subclonal events (lower VAF, potentially arising later):
  - KDM5C p.Y611* (VAF 22%) -- subclonal; may drive epigenetic heterogeneity
  - TP53 p.R248W (VAF 18%) -- subclonal, possibly enriched in sarcomatoid component
  - CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion -- likely subclonal or arising during aggressive clonal evolution; chromosomal deletion event
  - MET amplification (VAF interpretation limited by CN analysis) -- may represent a late-arising amplification driving the invasive/metastatic phenotype

Overall genomic complexity is high, consistent with a grade 4 tumor with sarcomatoid transformation. The combination of early epigenetic deregulation (VHL, PBRM1, BAP1, SETD2, KDM5C) followed by cell cycle dysregulation (CDKN2A/B deletion), telomere immortalization (TERT promoter), p53 inactivation/GOF, and MET amplification represents a stepwise progression from a clear cell RCC precursor to an aggressive, metastatic carcinoma with sarcomatoid features.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY TABLE
-------------------------------------------------------------------

Gene         | Alteration              | VAF   | Classification         | Tier
-------------|-------------------------|-------|------------------------|------
VHL          | p.L89fs*4 (frameshift)  | 48%   | Pathogenic, LOF        | I
PBRM1        | p.Q357* (nonsense)      | 42%   | Pathogenic, LOF        | I
BAP1         | p.R227* (nonsense)      | 39%   | Pathogenic, LOF        | I
MET          | Amplification (6.8x)    | --    | Pathogenic, GOF        | I
SETD2        | p.R1625* (nonsense)     | 31%   | Pathogenic, LOF        | II
TERT (prom.) | C228T                   | 35%   | Pathogenic, GOF        | II
CDKN2A/B     | Homozygous deletion     | --    | Pathogenic, LOF        | II
TP53         | p.R248W (missense)      | 18%   | Pathogenic, GOF        | II
KDM5C        | p.Y611* (nonsense)      | 22%   | Likely Pathogenic, LOF | II

TMB: 6.2 mut/Mb (LOW)
MSI Status: MSS
PD-L1 CPS (22C3): 18

-------------------------------------------------------------------
CLINICAL INTERPRETATION NOTE
-------------------------------------------------------------------
This comprehensive genomic profile is consistent with aggressive, high-grade clear cell RCC. The VHL frameshift mutation serves as the canonical initiating event, driving HIF-dependent oncogenesis and supporting the use of anti-angiogenic/VEGFR-targeted therapies. Concurrent BAP1 and PBRM1 loss -- while individually common in clear cell RCC -- together are associated with the aggressive grade 4, sarcomatoid phenotype observed in this patient. MET amplification is of particular therapeutic relevance, given the availability of cabozantinib as a MET/VEGFR/AXL multi-kinase inhibitor with demonstrated efficacy in RCC and possible enhanced benefit in MET-amplified tumors. The MSS, TMB-low profile does not support use of TMB-based immunotherapy indications, though the elevated PD-L1 CPS (18) and PBRM1 loss status may be consistent with benefit from checkpoint inhibitor-containing regimens (e.g., nivo/ipi) in the appropriate clinical context. Germline testing for BAP1 germline mutation syndrome (BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome; associated with uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, and RCC) should be discussed with the patient and genetic counseling offered, given the identified somatic BAP1 loss-of-function mutation -- though current analysis represents somatic sequencing with germline subtraction performed.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
REPORT ATTESTATION
-------------------------------------------------------------------
This report was generated using FoundationOne CDx (FDA-approved PMA P170019). Genomic alterations are reported based on the current scientific literature. This report is intended to be interpreted by qualified clinicians in the context of the patient's complete clinical history, pathological findings, and multidisciplinary team assessment. Genomic findings are subject to periodic re-interpretation as new evidence emerges.

Molecular Pathologist: [Signed, Foundation Medicine]
Medical Director Review: [Signed]
CAP/CLIA Laboratory Accredited: Yes
